segments.s299-g105: بدو = preposed indirect object ‘to him’; گفت is the past tense verb ‘said’ (here introducing direct speech); شاها is the vocative ‘O king’; به تیغ و سنان is an instrumental phrase ‘with sword and spear’ describing the means. The whole segment frames what is said to the king.
segments.s299-g106: کند is a 3rd-person present/jussive ‘let (him) do’ or ‘make’; هر کسی جنگ = ‘each person (do) battle’, with جنگ as the object; و پیچد عنان links a second verb (پیچد, 3sg present/subjunctive ‘twist/turn’) with عنان ‘reins’, giving a coordinated command (‘…and turn the reins’).
segments.s299-g107: پیاده به آید = literal ‘let him come on foot’ (پیاده as adverbial ‘on foot’, آید as 3sg present/jussive ‘come’); که introduces a purpose/result clause ‘so that’ and جوییم جنگ is 1st-plural present subjunctive ‘we may seek battle’ (جوییم = ‘seek, pursue’).
segments.s299-g108: به کردار شیران is an adverbial simile ‘in the manner/behavior of lions’ (به + کردار + plural شیران). بیازیم is 1st-plural present/subjunctive ‘let us burst forth/attack’ and takes چنگ ‘claw’ as its object — i.e. ‘we will strike forth with claws’.
segments.s299-g109: جهاندار خسرو = title + proper name ‘the sovereign Khosrow’; هم اندر زمان = ‘also/too at that time’ (هم = also, اندر زمان = in/at that moment). Together they set the subject and temporal adverbial.
segments.s299-g110: بدانست is the past tense verb ‘he perceived/knew’; اندیشه بدگمان is its object ‘a suspicious thought’ (اندیشه = thought/intention, بدگمان qualifying it). The verb precedes the object in this poetic order.
segments.s299-g111: به دل گفت = idiom ‘he said to his heart’ i.e. ‘he told himself’; کاین شیر introduces the clause ‘that this lion…’; با زور و چنگ is instrumental ‘by/with strength and claw’ describing the lion’s means or nature.
segments.s299-g112: نبیره فریدون و پور پشنگ are genealogical predicates with the copula omitted: ‘a scion/descendant of Fereydun and a son of Pashang’ (نبیره/پور as ‘offspring/son’ followed by proper names). The coordination gives two equivalent lineage labels.
segments.s299-g249: سپه را is the definite object fronted with the object marker را. همه modifies دل ("the whole/entire heart/spirits"). خروشان is an adjective used as an object/complement meaning "roaring/tumultuous." کنیم is the 1st person plural subjunctive/hortative of کردن (here "let us make/cause"), so the clause means "let us make the army's whole hearts roar."
segments.s299-g250: به آوردگه is a prepositional phrase marking destination (به + آوردگه = "to/into the arrival-place/venue"). بر سر افشان is an idiomatic sequence: بر سر ("upon/over the head/place") + افشان ("scattering/strewing"); together with کنیم (repeated hortative "let us do") it means "let us bring (them) to the hall and scatter (their heads) there." کنیم again is the 1st person plural hortative/optative.
segments.s299-g251: This is an archaic word order: ز خسرو نبد means "there was not (from) Khosrow." نبد is the negative past form "was not/did not exist." پیش از این is the standard phrase "before this." کینه چیز literally "a thing of hatred" = "any grudge/hatred." Altogether: "There was no grudge from Khosrow before this."
segments.s299-g252: کنون is the temporal adverb "now." بیفزود is the simple past "he added/increased." کینه بر کین is a doubled accusative/genitive phrase "hatred upon/over hatred" (repetition for emphasis). نیز means "also/too." The clause states that now he increased hatred even further: "now he added hatred upon hatred as well."
segments.s299-g253: سپه is the subject "the army/host." دل‌شکسته is a compound adjective meaning "broken‑hearted/despondent." شد is the past copula/verb "became." از بهر شاه (from/of-for the king) expresses cause/purpose: "because of/for the king." So: "The army became broken‑hearted on account of the king."
segments.s299-g254: خروشان و جوشان are coordinated adjectives placed before the noun, meaning "roaring and seething/boiling." همه qualifies رزمگاه as "the whole/entire battlefield/place of combat." The phrase describes the battlefield as tumultuous: "the whole battlefield roaring and seething."
segments.s299-g97: چو is an archaic temporal conjunction meaning "when." درمانده شد is a two-word predicate: درمانده (past-participial adjective 'distressed/at a loss') + شد (past-tense verb 'became'), so together 'became distressed.' با دل اندیشه کرد is a colloquial idiom: با دل ('with his heart' = inwardly) + اندیشه کرد (compound verb: noun اندیشه 'thought' + کردن 'to do' in past), meaning 'he pondered' or 'he thought to himself.'
segments.s299-g98: که‌گر is an archaic conditional conjunction meaning 'if.' شاه را is the definite object (شاه 'king' + را object marker). گویم here is the present-subjunctive/optative 1st-person form used in indirect thought ('(that) I should tell' / 'I should tell'), and اندر نبرد locates the action 'in the battle' (اندر = 'in/within', نبرد = 'battle'). The clause is a subordinate conditional/complement of his pondering.
segments.s299-g99: بیا is a hortative/imperative 'come' often used like 'let us.' تا introduces purpose/result here ('so that' / 'in order that'). به کشتی is the goal phrase ('to/into the ship'); پیاده شویم is a 1st-person plural subjunctive/periphrastic verb ('become on foot' → 'we disembark/go ashore') formed with the stem پیاده + شویم (شد#شو), so together 'let us get off (the ship).' Word order is typical Persian: hortative + purpose clause + prepositional goal + verb.
segments.s299-g100: ز introduces cause or source here ('by/on account of/from'), خوی هر دو literally 'the nature/disposition of both [of us]', and آهار داده شویم is a passive/periphrastic construction: آهار داده (given 'restraint/composure') + شویم (we become) → 'we shall be made composed/regulated.' The whole clause states the intended effect on both men's dispositions.
segments.s299-g101: پیاده نگردد uses پیاده 'on foot' + نگردد (negative present/subjunctive or generically 'should not become/go') — here 'should not go on foot' or 'it must not happen that he goes on foot.' که introduces a result/reason clause 'for/because' and عار آیدش is a simple present/future-style predicate 'shame will come to him' (عار 'shame' + آیدش 'comes to him').
segments.s299-g102: ز شاهی is 'from/because of his kingship' (ز = 'from/because of', شاهی = 'kingship/royal status'), تن خویش = 'his own body' (تن 'body' + خوی + ش split as root + possessive clitic), and خوار آیدش is 'will become despised' (خوار 'despised' + آیدش 'comes to him'). The clause gives the consequence: his regal status will still leave his person humiliated if he were to go on foot.
segments.s299-g103: بدین is a prepositional phrase 'by/with this' (here split as ب + دین in the word list; together = 'by this'), چاره‌گر 'remedy/finder of cure' modifies it. ز او نیابم رها is a negative present construction: ز او 'from him', نیابم (نیابم = negative or subjunctive of یافتن 'to find' in 1st person) 'I cannot find/obtain', and رها (predicate/adjective 'released/free') completes the idea 'I cannot find release from him.' Word order is literary: instrument/means + source + first-person verb + predicate.
segments.s299-g104: شدم is past 'I became' (first person past of شدن), بی‌گمان is an adverb 'undoubtedly,' در دم is a locative phrase 'in the breath/moment' (در = 'in', دم = 'breath' or 'moment' — here literal 'breath' of the dragon), and اژدها 'dragon' is the possessor of دم. The clause states the poet-speaker's fate: he was, without doubt, in the dragon's breath (i.e., in imminent peril).
segments.s299-g177: گرفتش is گرفت + enclitic object ش (he seized him); Persian places the verb before the object-clause here. به introduces the locator ('by/on' the specified part), so به چپ گردن و راست پشت lists two body-points in parallel (چپ and راست modify گردن and پشت respectively). و simply connects the two noun phrases.
segments.s299-g178: برآورد is here a past tense of برآوردن (he lifted up, brought up), connecting to گرفتَش (he seized him and then lifted him). و links the next action. زد is simple past 'he struck'; بر + زمین gives the target 'upon the ground'; بر درشت is an adverbial phrase ('with force, harshly/roughly') modifying زد. (Note: برآوردن 'to lift' is the sense used here.)
segments.s299-g179: همه modifies مهره (all the vertebrae). مهره پشت او is a genitival phrase 'the vertebrae of his back'. همچو نی is a simile 'like a reed' placed after the noun it describes (typical Persian order: noun + همچو + simile).
segments.s299-g180: شد is past 'became' (resultative). از درد introduces cause ('from/because of pain'); ریزان is a present-participial/ adjective meaning 'scattering / falling apart' describing the state of the vertebrae; و links the next clause. بگسست is past passive/ intransitive 'it/they broke/separated' and پی here refers to the continuity/connection (the backbone/sequence) which 'broke' — this uses a compact poetic phrase where the cause (pain) leads to the breaking apart.
segments.s299-g181: یکی here means 'one / someone' (an unspecified person). تیغ تیز is a noun phrase 'a sharp sword' (noun + adjective). از introduces origin or manner, and کشید is the past of کشیدن; in this combat context کشید means 'drew' (as in drew a sword) or 'pulled out'—the object (the sword) is implied as being drawn into action. میان‌بر is a compact poetic compound whose literal dictionary senses ('shortcut, expedient') don't fit well here; read in context as part of the verbal phrase describing how the sword was drawn ('drew [it] from the middle/with a middle-stroke') — its exact idiomatic sense is archaic/poetic.
segments.s299-g182: سراسر (throughout/utterly) modifies the following object دل نامور 'the famed heart'. بر here is the preposition used with درید (tore) to indicate the action upon the heart; درید is past 'tore' (perfective). The clause therefore states the sword's action: it tore the famous heart completely.
segments.s299-g183: بر او کرد جوشن is literally 'he made/placed a coat-of-mail upon him' or more idiomatically 'his mail upon him (was)'. کرد is the past of 'to do/make' and here helps present the state of the armor as acted upon; همه چاک چاک is a doubled adjective 'entirely gashed/rent to pieces' following the noun it qualifies (joushan). In short, the armor is described as completely torn.
segments.s299-g184: همی ریخت is a past/continuous poetic construction meaning 'was pouring / was falling' (همی often marks ongoing aspect in older Persian). بر تارک = 'upon (the) crown/nape (of his head)' (تارک here denotes the head's crown), از درد introduces cause 'from pain', and خاک is the thing falling (dust/earth). Together: 'dust fell upon his crown from (the intensity of) pain.'
segments.s299-g65: کنون (an adverb) is fronted to set time ('now'). آرزو کن is an imperative construction: آرزو (noun/verb 'wish') + the imperative کن ('do!'), meaning 'make a wish' or 'ask for'. یکی رزمگاه is the direct object: یکی (quantifier 'one / a certain') premodifies رزمگاه ('battlefield'), so the phrase means 'one battlefield' or 'a single battlefield'.
segments.s299-g66: به introduces a purposive or locative phrase ('for / as / to'), here linking to دیدار ('meeting, rendezvous'). دور is a postpositive adjective modifying دیدار ('a distant meeting'). از میان سپاه is a prepositional phrase 'from the midst of the army' (از = 'from', میان = 'midst/middle', سپاه = 'host/army'), indicating separation from the main force.
segments.s299-g67: نهادند is a 3rd-person plural past tense verb 'they set/established' (here 'they made/laid down'). پیمان is the object 'a pact/covenant'. که introduces the content clause ('that ...'). از هر دو روی is a prepositional locution: از = 'from', هر دو = 'both / either', and روی here means 'side' ('from both sides').
segments.s299-g68: به یاری نیاید is a negative jussive/subjunctive sense 'may/shall not come to help' where به یاری ('to the aid') is an adverbial phrase and نیاید is the negative form of آید. کسی is the indefinite subject 'anyone' and کینه‌جوی postmodifies it ('vengeful, revenge-seeking'), so the clause means 'that no revenge-seeking person come to aid.'
segments.s299-g69: هم adds emphasis or 'also.' اینها که دارند با ما درفش is a relative clause: اینها ('these, those') + که ('who') + دارند ('they have/hold', present tense) + با ما ('with us') + درفش ('banner/standard'), yielding 'those who carry the banner with us.'
segments.s299-g70: ز is the causal preposition 'because of / from.' بد روی is a compound idea 'bad face / ill countenance / ill disposition' (بد modifies روی). ایشان is the genitival/possessive 'their / they', and نگردد is a negative present/jussive form of گشتن 'to become' (here 'shall not become' or 'will not turn'). بنفش is the predicative adjective 'purple.' The sequence means 'because of their ill countenance their banner shall not turn purple.'
segments.s299-g71: برفتند is past tense 3rd-person plural 'they went/they departed.' هر دو is an idiomatic expression meaning 'both.' ز لشکر is 'from the army' (ز = 'from', لشکر = 'host/army'). به دور is an adverbial phrase 'to a distance / off into the distance.' The verb+subject+adjunct order (برفتند هر دو ز لشکر) is typical of classical/poetic Persian.
segments.s299-g72: چنانچون introduces a simile 'just as / like.' شود is a present/generic form of شدن 'become/happen' used here to set the parallel. مرد شادان is 'a man (mard) joyful' (noun + adjective), and به سور means 'at a feast' (به = locative/at, سور = banquet/feast). Together: 'just as a man becomes joyful at a feast.'
segments.s299-g121: اگر starts a conditional ('if'). پای بر خاک is a prepositional phrase with پای (direct object 'foot') + بر 'on' + خاک 'earth/ground'. باید نهاد is a modal + verbal stem: باید 'ought/must' plus نهاد (the past stem of نهادن 'to put/set') forming a necessity 'must set/put'; in older/poetic Persian the past stem follows باید to express the required action.
segments.s299-g122: من is the explicit first‑person subject. از تخم کشواد is an ablative/genitive phrase 'from the seed/stock of Keshvad' (تخم = origin/seed + کشواد a proper name). دارم نژاد places the possession verb دارم 'I have' before the noun نژاد 'lineage/race' — a common poetic order meaning 'I am of the race/of the lineage of...'.
segments.s299-g123: بمان is an imperative ('remain/stay'). تا introduces a temporal/purpose clause 'until', and شوم is the first‑person singular subjunctive/present ('I may become'). پیش او is a locative/relational phrase 'in his presence/before him'. جنگ‌ساز is a predicative nominal 'one who makes or wages war' — the role the speaker aims to attain.
segments.s299-g124: نه is a negation/contrastive particle ('not'). شاه جهاندار گردن‌فراز is a nominal phrase with stacked epithets: شاه 'king', جهاندار 'world‑ruler/sovereign', گردن‌فراز 'proud, lofty‑necked'; together they are negated by نه to contrast the desired role (جنگ‌ساز) with being a proud sovereign.
segments.s299-g125: The clause follows classical VSO flexibility: به رهام 'to Roham' is the indirect object, گفت is the verb 'said' (past tense), and آن زمان شهریار is a temporal expression + subject 'then the king' placed after the verb. In older Persian the subject often follows the verb, so the line reads 'then the king said to Roham.'
segments.s299-g126: که‌ای here is an exclamatory/vocative particle (classical 'که ای' ≈ 'O') addressing the addressee. مهربان is an adjective 'kind', and پهلوان سوار is a compound noun 'champion/hero on horseback' (پهلوان 'hero/contestant' + سوار 'rider'). The sequence is a vocative: 'O kind champion-rider...'.
segments.s299-g127: چو introduces a comparison/simile ('as' or 'like'). شیده دلاور is a nominal phrase where شیده is a name/identifier and دلاور is an epithet 'brave/valiant'. ز تخم پشنگ is a poetic genitive 'from the seed/stock of Pashang' (ز = from, تخم = seed/stock, پشنگ = proper name), indicating lineage.
segments.s299-g128: چنان دان is an imperative phrase 'consider/think him thus' (چنان = so/thus, دان = know/consider). که introduces the subordinate clause giving the content of that consideration. با تو نیاید به جنگ is a negated clause 'he will not come to fight with you' where نیاید is a negative present/subjunctive 'does not/should not come', با تو 'against/with you', and به جنگ 'to battle'.
segments.s299-g161: ولیکن is a conjunctive adverb meaning 'but/yet' and introduces a contrast. ترا‌گر is a fused poetic conditional ('اگر ترا' → 'if to you / if you'), placing the condition on the addressee. چنینست is a present copular predicate ('it is thus' / 'it is so') and کام is the noun 'desire/wish' governed by that predicate — together giving 'if such is your desire.'
segments.s299-g162: نپیچم is negative + present-subjunctive/jussive of پیچیدن here meaning 'I will not turn/deviate' (first person singular); the negative prefix ن combined with the present-form expresses refusal/not doing. ز رای تو is a prepositional phrase 'from your counsel' (ز = 'from', رای = 'counsel/plan', تو = 'you') that marks what the speaker will not depart from. هرگز is the adverb 'never' (emphatic), and لگام ('the reins/bridle') is a concrete metaphorical object for guidance/restraint — overall: 'I will never turn the reins away from your counsel.'
segments.s299-g163: فرود آمد is a compound past-expression meaning 'alighted/dismounted' (فرود + آمد = 'came down'). از اسب is the prepositional phrase 'from the horse', شبرنگ is a postpositive adjective modifying اسب ('variegated/colored'), and شاه is the subject placed at the end — a common poetic word order (verb + complements + subject).
segments.s299-g164: ز سر is a prepositional phrase 'from the head' (ز = 'from', سر = 'head'). برگرفت is a past verb 'he took up/removed', followed by the direct object آن کیانی کلاه ('that Kayanian cap') where کیانی is an epithet 'Kayanian/royal' modifying کلاه. The subject (the king) is understood from the previous clause.
segments.s299-g165: به رهام داد is a verb phrase where به introduces the indirect object/recipient ('to Roham') and داد is the past tense 'gave'. آن گرانمایه اسب is the direct object NP ('that precious/esteemed horse') with demonstrative آن and adjective گرانمایه preceding the head اسب (poetic order).
segments.s299-g166: پیاده is an adverb/adjectival phrase 'on foot' (describing how he arrived). بیامد is the past tense 'came' (note poetic inversion: the subject 'Roham' is not repeated), چو is the simile conjunction 'like/as', and آذرگشسب is a proper name used as the comparison standard — 'he came on foot like Azar-Gashasp.'
segments.s299-g167: چو functions as the temporal conjunction 'when' here. پیاده and از دور together set the scene ('on foot' and 'from afar'), and دیدش is the past verb 'saw him' with the enclitic third-person object -ش. پشنگ is the subject (proper name). The phrase reads 'When Pashang saw him from afar on foot...' with poetic word order bringing circumstances forward.
segments.s299-g168: فرود آمد is the past 'alighted/dismounted'. از باره جنگی is a prepositional phrase 'from his war-burden/war-gear' (باره here = 'load/burden', جنگی = 'warlike/for war'), and پلنگ is an appositive/epithet 'the panther' applied to the warrior — painters often use animal epithets poetically. Together: 'he alighted from his war-gear like a panther.'
segments.s299-g241: همی is a classical adverb here meaning 'continually/kept on' and combines with the past verb to give a repeated or continuous past action. ریخت is the past root meaning 'fell/flowed' (here the sense 'pouring/flow'); the prepositional phrase از دیده خونین سرشک ('from the eye, blood-stained teardrop') follows to mark source: دیده = 'eye' and خونین modifies it as 'blood-stained' (literally 'bloody'), while سرشک is the noun 'teardrop'. Word order is poetic but straightforward: adverb + verb + source phrase.
segments.s299-g242: ز introduces cause or source ('from/because of') and دردی is the indefinite 'a pain'. که is the relative pronoun 'which/that' linking the clause; درمان is 'a remedy/cure' and نداند is an archaic 3rd-person negative form of دانستن ('knows not')—in narrative it functions like 'knew no cure' or 'could find no cure'. پزشک at the end is the subject of نداند ('the physician/doctor').
segments.s299-g243: همه is the universal quantifier 'all/everyone' placed before the noun. نامداران is a plural substantive meaning 'renowned/illustrious ones'. پاسخ‌گزار here is an epithet meaning 'answering/ready to reply' (an adjectival or attributive compound) describing those men.
segments.s299-g244: زبان literally 'tongue' is used metonymically for 'speech' or 'utterance' ('opened their tongues' = 'spoke'). برگشادند is the past plural ('they opened' or 'they revealed'), a compound with the prefix بر. بر before شهریار functions as a dative/benefactive 'to/toward the king' (here 'addressed the king'). The sequence is idiomatic: they opened their mouths and spoke to the king.
segments.s299-g245: که introduces a content/relative clause ('that'). این دادگر is 'this just one' with دادگر functioning as 'just ruler/judge' (subject). بر تو ('toward you' / 'for you') marks the beneficiary; آسان is a predicate adjective 'easy' ('to make easy'), and کناد is an archaic 3rd-person optative/subjunctive form meaning 'may do' or 'would do' (classical equivalent of modern کند). Together it says 'that this just ruler may make it easy for you.'
segments.s299-g246: بداندیش 'the ill-intentioned' is fronted and followed by the object marker را marking its definiteness/role; دل 'heart' is the thing affected and هراسان is an adjective meaning 'frightened/terrified'. The final کناد repeats the optative 'may do', so literally 'may make the heart of the ill-intentioned frightened' (i.e., may terrify the malicious).
segments.s299-g247: ز ما 'of us' (among us/as for us) with نیز 'also' yields 'we too'. یک تن is an idiomatic 'one person' (i.e., 'not even one person'). نسازد is the negative form of ساختن/سازیدن here meaning 'will make' or 'will cause'—in context 'will not make (a) pause'—so the phrase means 'not one of us will pause/cease'. درنگ is the noun 'pause/delay'. The verb's archaic form signals negated obligation or present-future meaning in the narrative.
segments.s299-g248: شب و روز 'night and day' is a standard adverbial phrase ('constantly'). بر here is the preposition 'upon/with regard to' and links to the nouns that follow: درد used in the sense of emotional suffering or sorrow, and کین 'vengeance/hatred'. پشنگ is a proper name (Pashang), the possessor: 'Pashang's pain and vengeance' (i.e., we will be occupied with Pashang's sorrow and revenge).
segments.s299-g25: سپهدار is the subject ('the commander'); با introduces accompaniment or means ('with'); the coordinated nouns جوشن و گرز name the equipment (cuirass and mace). The final خود emphasizes that he acted personally — 'himself' (a poetic/archaic emphatic use). Word order places the modifiers after the subject in a compressed appositive phrase.
segments.s299-g26: به introduces the indirect object ('to/for the army'); لشکر is that object. فرستاد is a simple past transitive verb 'sent/dispatched' (3rd sg.). چندی qualifies the direct object (an adverb/noun meaning 'a few'/'several'), and درود is the thing sent ('greetings' or salutations). The sequence is Subject (previous segment) + به + indirect object + verb + quantity + direct object.
segments.s299-g27: که introduces the subordinate/result clause ('so that'/'that'); یک تن = 'one person' (here idiomatically 'not a single man'); مجنبید is a negative-stem form meaning 'did not stir/move' (classical/poetic negative form); ز (= از) means 'from' and این رزمگاه specifies the place ('this battlefield'). The clause states a result/state: no one moved from the field.
segments.s299-g28: A coordinated list of the army's parts: چپ و راست و قلب و جناح — 'left and right and heart (center) and wing(s)'; سپاه at the end is the head noun governing the list (the genitive/possessive relation: 'the army's ...'). This is a nominal string enumerating the army's sectors with no intervening verb.
segments.s299-g29: نباید is an impersonal modal/negative meaning 'it must not / one should not'; که introduces the subordinate clause; جوید (subjunctive/present) means 'seek/desire' and follows the negation; کسی is 'anyone' (indefinite subject). جنگ و جوش are coordinated nouns 'war/battle and ardor/passion' — the objects sought. The construction = 'No one should seek war and ardor.'
segments.s299-g30: به marks the target of attention ('to' or 'towards'); رهام گودرز are proper names and together function as the objects of listening. دارید is the present/plural form of 'to have' used idiomatically with گوش ('have ear') to mean 'lend an ear' or 'pay attention' — an exhortation in the 2nd-person plural. گوش here means 'attention/listening' rather than the physical ear.
segments.s299-g31: چو = 'when'; خورشید is the subject 'the sun'; بر چرخ is a prepositional phrase 'upon the wheel/sky' (چرخ = heaven/sky, a common poetic word); گردد (from گشتن) is a verbal copula meaning 'becomes/turns' and here combines with بلند ('high') to mean 'becomes high' or 'rises high'. The clause sets the temporal condition: 'when the sun rises high in the heavens.'
segments.s299-g32: ببینید is a 2nd-person-plural imperative/polite present 'see/look'; تا here functions as 'so that/to (find out)'; بر is the preposition 'upon/against'; که introduces the indirect question/relative 'who'; آید is 'comes/befalls' (3rd sg.); گزند is 'harm' or injury. Together: 'Look (and see) to whom harm will come.'
segments.s299-g201: This is a short imperative/vocative phrase with poetic word order. 'به من' (to me) places the beneficiary before the verb; 'بر' is a prepositional/ directional particle placed before the verb (classical/poetic usage) and here links the action to the speaker; 'ببخشای' is an older 3rd-person imperative/optative form meaning 'forgive' or 'grant forgiveness'; 'شاها' is a vocative 'O king'; 'به مهر' is a prepositional phrase 'with kindness' (preposition before the noun).
segments.s299-g202: 'که' introduces a subordinate (optative/result) clause: 'that'/'so that'. 'از جان تو' is a genitive/agentive phrase 'from your life' (literally 'by/through your life'); 'شاد' is a predicative adjective 'be glad/happy'; 'بادا' is the classical optative particle meaning 'may it be' (wishes); 'سپهر' is the subject/scene 'the heavens' placed after the verb phrase in poetic order.
segments.s299-g203: 'بدو گفت شاه' is a compact word order: 'بدو' ('to him') fronted, then 'گفت' (said, past) and then the subject 'شاه' (the king) — a common classical inversion. 'آنچه دیدی ز من' is an embedded clause 'whatever you saw of me' (آنچه = 'that which/whatever', دیدی = 2nd-person past 'you saw', ز من = 'concerning/from me').
segments.s299-g204: 'نیا را بگوی' is an instruction: 'tell Niyâ' where 'نیا' (proper name or kin-term) is marked by the object/topic marker 'را' and 'بگوی' is the imperative 'tell' (classical vocative/imperative form). 'اندر آن انجمن' is a locative prepositional phrase 'in/within that assembly' (اندر = in/within, آن انجمن = that assembly). Note that the verb 'بگوی' appears after the object+را in this poetic word order.
segments.s299-g205: 'زمین را ببوسید' is a transitive past clause 'he kissed the earth' with the definite object marker 'را' after زمین. The coordinating 'و' links the next clause. 'کرد آفرین' literally 'made acclamation' — a fixed expression meaning 'he exclaimed
segments.s299-g206: 'بسیچید' is a past-tense verb meaning 'he/they mustered/gathered' (poetic past form). 'ره' (way/road) plus the directional 'سوی' introduces destination 'toward'. 'سالار چین' is a genitive/title phrase 'the lord/commander of China' (سالار = commander/leader, چین = China). Word order is verb + object (ره سوی ...) in a compact classical style.
segments.s299-g207: This opening prepositional phrase gives source/background: 'وز آن دشت' functions as 'from that plain' (وز is a poetic variant of از here). 'کیخسرو' is the subject, and 'کینه‌جوی' is an adjectival/epithet 'seeking vengeance' modifying him (postnominal epithet is typical in epic style).
segments.s299-g208: 'سوی لشکر خویش بنهاد روی' is a verb-centered clause: 'سوی' = 'toward', 'لشکر خویش' = 'his own army' (here 'خوی' + the clitic 'ش' together form 'خویش' = 'own/his kin' in two tokens), and 'بنهاد روی' is a classical periphrasis meaning 'he set/placed his face' i.e. 'he turned his face toward' (بنهاد = put/set; روی = face). The phrase thus expresses motion/direction of attention or turning toward his army.
segments.s299-g81: سواران is the plural subject 'the horsemen/riders'. چو is a simile marker here ('like/as'). شیران is the simile's noun 'lions'. اخته زهار is a two-word descriptive phrase after the simile; both words are archaic/poetic modifiers attached to شیران (functioning like epithets). The whole segment reads: 'the horsemen, like lions [epithet ...]'.
segments.s299-g82: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause ('who/that'). باشند is 3rd person plural present-subjunctive/optative form of بودن used in narrative to state their condition ('are' / 'may be'); here it functions as simple present-state. پر + از is the 'full of' construction (پرِ از X). خشم is the noun 'anger/fury' and روز شکار is a genitive phrase 'the day of the hunt' (روز = day, شکار = hunt). The clause means 'who are full of fury on the day of the hunt.'
segments.s299-g83: بگشتند is the past 3rd-person-plural verb 'they turned/they returned'. با is the preposition 'with'. نیزه‌های دراز is a noun phrase 'long spears' (نیزه‌های = spears, دراز = long) where دراز postmodifies the noun.
segments.s299-g84: چو again introduces a simile 'as/like'. خورشید is the simile's noun 'the sun'. تابنده is a present participle/adjective 'shining/radiant'. گشت is the past 3rd-person-singular of گشتن used here as 'became'/'turned (to be)'. از فراز is a prepositional phrase 'from on high' (از = from, فراز = summit/above). Grammatically this is a subordinate simile clause modifying how they turned.
segments.s299-g85: نماند is a past-tense verb meaning 'was left/ remained' used here in the negative construction (no + remain). ایچ is an emphatic negative 'nothing/none'. بر is the locative preposition 'on/upon'. نیزه‌هاشان is the possessed noun 'their spears' (plural + possessive). سنان refers to the spear-points/heads. Word order places the negated existential first: 'Nothing remained on their spears' points.'
segments.s299-g86: پر از + noun is the 'full of' construction: پر از آب = 'full of water' (in epic poetry آب can stand metonymically for blood/ichor). برگستوان and عنان are two nouns coordinated by و. Grammatically this segment lists locations/things that are full of that liquid ('on the haft/arm and the reins').
segments.s299-g87: به is a preposition here ('against' / 'to' / 'upon' depending on context). رومی is an adjective/noun 'Roman/Byzantine' modifying the following noun. عمود can mean 'pillar/column' (a tactical formation or literally a column); then the coordination 'و به شمشیر و تیر' lists other means ('and with sword and arrow'). The prepositional pattern repeats before شمشیر to mark instruments.
segments.s299-g88: بگشتند again is the past 3rd-plural 'they turned/returned'. با یکدگر is a prepositional phrase 'with one another' (با = with, یکدگر = one another). ناگزیر is an adjective/adverb 'compelled/inevitable' describing the manner or necessity of their turning (i.e., they were obliged/forced to do so).
segments.s299-g1: چو here is the temporal conjunctive 'when' introducing a circumstantial clause. روشن شد is the past verbal phrase 'became bright' (روشن + شد, with شد functioning as the copular/predicative 'became'). آن چادر لاژورد is the noun phrase 'that lapis (blue) tent' (demonstrative آن before the noun; لاژورد modifies چادر). The clause order (temporal marker + verb + subject) is common in classical Persian poetry.
segments.s299-g2: جهان شد is the main clause: 'the world became' (شد = 'became'). به کردار is a prepositional phrase meaning 'in the manner/appearance of' (کردار used figuratively as 'manner/guise'). یاقوت زرد is the noun phrase 'yellow ruby' that describes the new appearance. The verb شد links the subject جهان to the predicative phrase introduced by به کردار.
segments.s299-g3: نشست is the simple past 'he sat/ alighted' (here 'alighted' from a mount). از بر is a poetic compound meaning 'from off' or 'from upon' (از + بر). اسب جنگی پشنگ is the noun phrase 'the war-horse Pashang' where جنگی ('warlike') qualifies اسب and پشنگ is the horse's name placed after it in classical diction.
segments.s299-g4: ز here is the poetic preposition 'from/by/with'. باد جوانی literally 'the wind/breath of youth' is an idiom meaning 'youthful impulse/vigor'. سرش is 'his head' (سر + enclitic ش = his). پر ز جنگ is 'full of war' (پر = 'full (of)'; ز جنگ = 'of battle/war'). The whole phrase describes his youthful ardor filled with the spirit of combat.
segments.s299-g5: به جوشن بپوشید is a past clause 'he put on a cuirass' (به marks the object/recipient, جوشن = 'cuirass/armor', بپوشید = past 'he put on/was clothed'). روشن modifies جوشن ('bright/gleaming cuirass'). برش here is functioning as 'on him' in context (classical compression), but the provided lemma senses for برش do not match this locative/possessive use (see senses field).
segments.s299-g6: ز آهن literally 'of iron' (ز = 'from/of' in poetic register, آهن = 'iron'), کلاه کیان = 'the Kian (royal) helmet' (کلاه = helmet/hat, کیان = 'the kings/royal house', here a genitive/epithet), بر سرش = 'on his head' (بر = 'on', سر = 'head', ش = enclitic 'his'). The phrase states the helmet of the Kians (made of iron) was on his head.
segments.s299-g7: درفشش ('darfash' with the enclitic) is 'his banner/standard' (the form includes the possessive suffix). یکی here functions as 'a certain' (indefinite introducer). ترک جنگی is 'a Turk, warlike' (ترک = Turk, جنگی = 'warlike/warrior' modifying ترک). به چنگ is a prepositional phrase 'in (his) hand' using چنگ ('claw' or figuratively 'hand/grip') to indicate something held; word order places the banner and the man in apposition before the following verb.
segments.s299-g8: خرامان is an adverbial participle/adverb meaning 'with a proud/stately gait' placed before the verb. بیامد is the past 'he came'. به سان = 'like/as' (a simile marker), and پلنگ is 'leopard'. The clause as a whole describes his manner of approach—he came striding like a leopard.
segments.s299-g113: گر is the conditional particle 'if'. آسوده is a predicative adjective 'at ease/relieved' and گرد(د) is the classical third‑person subjunctive/present ('becomes'); the subject تن 'the body' appears after the predicate (inverted order for emphasis). آسان کند is a verb phrase 'makes (it) easy' with کند in the same subjunctive/present form; together the clause reads literally 'If the body becomes at ease, (he) makes (it) easy.'
segments.s299-g114: بسی here is an intensive adverb meaning 'very/much'. شیر دل is a compound 'lion‑hearted' (شیر + دل) with را marking it as the (definite) object. هراسان کند is a causative construction 'makes (someone) frightened' where کند is the third‑person present/subjunctive; word order places the intensifier and object before the verb for emphasis.
segments.s299-g115: اگر introduces a conditional clause. من is the first‑person subject. پیاده نگردم uses نگردم (classical subjunctive/negative of گردیدن 'become/go') with پیاده 'on foot' — together 'if I do not go/dismount on foot'. به جنگ marks direction/purpose 'into/for battle' (به + noun).
segments.s299-g116: به ایرانیان is an indirect object 'for/to the Iranians' placed before the verb. بر کند is a verbal complex where بر (upon/against) combines with کند 'he makes/does' to mean 'he puts/sets (against)'. جای تنگ is the direct object 'a narrow/tight place' (جای + adjective تنگ). The clause therefore means 'he will put the Iranians in a tight place.'
segments.s299-g117: بدو is the short form meaning 'to him' (prepositional adverb). گفت is the past tense 'said' (classical narrative past). رهام is the subject (Roham) following the verb, and کای تاجور is a vocative: کای is an archaic 'O' and تاجور 'Tajur' (addressed person/title). The whole segment is 'Roham said to him, “O Tajur...”'.
segments.s299-g118: بدین کار is 'by/with this deed' (بدین = به + این). ننگی مگردان is a negative imperative: ننگی (disgraceful/disgrace) + مگردان 'do not make/turn into' (م‑ as negative + گردان). گهر is the object 'the jewel' (what must not be disgraced). Word order places the instrument/condition and the negative imperative before the object for rhetorical emphasis: 'do not bring disgrace on the jewel by this deed.'
segments.s299-g119: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. خسرو is the subject (Khosrow). پیاده کند is 'dismounts / goes on foot' with کند as the narrative/subjunctive present. کارزار names the purpose or scene 'for battle'/'battlefield' (it can be read as 'dismounts for battle' or 'dismounts to the battlefield'). The clause sets a hypothetical/temporal frame 'When Khosrow dismounts to fight...'.
segments.s299-g120: چه باید introduces a rhetorical question 'what can/should (one) do?'. بر این دشت is a locative phrase 'on this plain' (بر = on/upon, این دشت = this plain). چندین سوار 'several/many horsemen' is the agent referenced; note the main verb of their action is left implicit after باید, a frequent poetic omission: 'what can many horsemen do on this plain?'.
segments.s299-g217: سواری is the subject ('a rider/horseman'). همی is an archaic continuous/emphatic particle often used with past verbs in Ferdowsi to mark a vivid or ongoing action; here it colours شد. شد is a past/historical verb meaning 'happened/appeared'. بر + آن ریگ نرم is a prepositional phrase 'on that soft sand' with the preposition بر before the demonstrative آن and the noun ریگ modified by the adjective نرم that follows it.
segments.s299-g218: This is a sequence of descriptive phrases: برهنه سر is an adjective + noun ('bare‑headed') with the adjective before the noun. و links the next coordinated phrase, دیده پر خون و گرم, where دیده ('eye/eyes') is followed by the post‑nominal predicate پر (here 'full (of)') governing the genitive complement خون ('blood'); the final و گرم adds another predicative quality 'and hot' that also modifies the eyes.
segments.s299-g219: بیامد is a past tense verb 'he came'. به نزدیک is a prepositional/locative construction meaning 'to/near' with به + نزدیک, and افراسیاب is the proper‑name object of that locative phrase ('to/near Afrasiab'). Word order follows classical Persian: verb then locative phrase.
segments.s299-g220: دل is the semantic subject/experiencer 'heart'; از درد ('from/because of pain') gives the cause; خسته is a predicative adjective 'weary' agreeing with دل and functioning without an explicit copula. دو دیده ('two eyes') is a numeral + noun, followed by the adjective phrase پر آب where پر is a post‑nominal predicate meaning 'full (of)' and آب ('water') stands for tears in this context.
segments.s299-g221: برآورد is a classical/periphrastic verb meaning 'brought forth / caused to come out' (here used in past narrative). پوشیده راز is the object phrase 'the hidden/covered secret' with پوشیده as adjective before the noun راز. از نهفت is an ablative phrase 'from concealment/from hiding' modifying the action of bringing out.
segments.s299-g222: همه ('all, everything') is the direct object of بگفت ('he told/said'); پیش سالار ترکان is a prepositional‑like phrase meaning 'in the presence of / before the commander of the Turks' with پیش functioning as 'before/in the presence of' followed by the title سالار and the group noun ترکان. بگفت is an archaic past form 'he said / he told'.
segments.s299-g223: جهاندار is the subject 'the sovereign' or ruler. گشت is a past auxiliary/verb here meaning 'became' (subject underwent a change). از جهان ناامید is a prepositional phrase where از marks the source/reason and ناامید is a predicate adjective 'hopeless', so together 'became hopeless of the world'.
segments.s299-g224: بکند is a classical perfective verb form 'he did / he pulled/removed' in the narrative (here used to mean he plucked or tore out). آن is a demonstrative 'that' marking the object. چو is a simile marker 'like/as', so چو کافور qualifies موی سپید: 'that white hair like camphor' (کافور = camphor, a whitish substance). موی سپید is the noun + adjective 'white hair' with the adjective following the noun as in Persian.
segments.s299-g41: میان is a preposition 'between' that begins the locative phrase; دو صف is a cardinal + noun 'two ranks/lines' and شیده is a postpositive adjective (classical word order) meaning 'fierce/mad' modifying صف. او را is the object pronoun with the definite object marker را, and بدید is the past tense (archaic/poetic) form 'saw' (subject is understood from context).
segments.s299-g42: یکی functions as an indefinite/indeterminate 'a certain' or 'one (thereupon)'; باد سرد is the noun phrase 'cold wind'; از جگر is a prepositional phrase 'from (the) jigar' where جگر poetically means 'heart/seat of feeling'; بر کشید is a compound verbal idea 'برکشیدن' used in past tense meaning 'drew up / rose from' — an archaic, pictorial verb of motion.
segments.s299-g43: بدو گفت is the light-verb construction meaning 'said to him' (بدو = 'to him/unto him', گفت = 'said' in context though the provided dictionary senses are nominal); پور سیاوش is the subject 'the son of Siyâvash' that follows the verb (permissible word order in epic poetry). رد here begins the reported utterance and functions as an imperative/exhortation in the speech (rendered in translation as the vocative/address that follows).
segments.s299-g44: توی‌ای is the poetic vocative form 'تو ای' (= 'O you'); پسندیده is an adjectival epithet 'chosen/pleasant/esteemed'; پرخرد is a compound adjective 'full of wisdom / very wise' used here as direct address. Together they form the addressee's title in the quoted speech.
segments.s299-g45: This is a genitive/appositive noun cluster: نبیره 'scion/offspring' modified by جهاندار 'world‑ruler' and توران 'of Turan', with سپاه 'host/army' completing the chain — i.e. 'a scion of the sovereign of Turan's host.' Word order places titles and place-names after the head noun in classical style.
segments.s299-g46: که introduces a relative clause modifying the preceding noun. ساید with همی is an archaic/poetic verbal phrase indicating ongoing/state ('is/ever is'); ترگ is the noun 'rag/shred' (here metonymically 'standard' or 'tattered banner'); بر چرخ ماه is a locative phrase 'upon the wheel of the sky/moon' (چرخ = heaven/sky, ماه = moon/beautiful one). The clause describes the constant sight of that rag on the heavenly wheel.
segments.s299-g47: جز آنی که is a concessive phrase 'except that' (آنی here introducing the exception). بر تو گمانی برد is an idiomatic sequence meaning 'a suspicion/doubt came upon you' (گمانی = 'a supposition/doubt'; برد here is the verbal element 'took/brought' in the classical idiom 'گمانی بردن' = 'to take/suffer a suspicion').
segments.s299-g48: جهاندیده‌ای is a descriptive epithet 'a worldly‑wise one' (with the -ی ending forming an agent/character noun). کو here introduces a relative clause ('who/which' in older usage), and خرد پرورد is the verb phrase 'nurtured/raised wisdom' (پرورد is the past/formal form of پروردن 'to nurture' and خرد = 'wisdom'). Together: 'a worldly‑wise man who reared wisdom.'
segments.s299-g89: زمین شد is literal 'the ground became' (زمین + past of شدن). ز introduces the cause ('from/by'), گرد here means the dust/throng raised by movement, and سواران سیاه is the noun plus postposed adjective 'horsemen black' (poetic word order). Together: 'the ground became (dark) from the dust of the black horsemen.'
segments.s299-g90: نگشتند is the negative past, 3rd-person plural of گشتن ('they did not go/roam/turn'). سیر here has the idiomatic sense of 'satiated/ceasing' (i.e. 'become satisfied/stop'), and اندر آوردگاه is an adverbial phrase 'in/around the battlefield' (اندر = in/within + آوردگاه = battlefield). Word order places the verb before the final locative phrase.
segments.s299-g91: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. شیده دل ('shideh del') is a compound adjective 'heart-struck/afflicted', and زور خسرو places 'heart and strength' before the possessor 'Khosrow' (possession expressed by juxtaposition in poetic diction). بدید is simple past 'he saw'. Together: 'When he saw Khosrow's stricken heart and might...'
segments.s299-g92: ز = 'from/on account of'. مژگان = 'eyelashes' (genitive source), سرشکش = 'tear‑drop' (subject), به رخ = 'onto the cheek/face' (به = toward/onto + رخ = face), and برچکید is an intransitive perfective meaning 'fell/dripped forth' (here 'sprang/fell'). The clause describes tears falling from the lashes onto the face.
segments.s299-g93: بدانست is past 'he knew/realized'. کان is an archaic demonstrative 'that' (classical/didactic register). فره ایزدیست = 'farr (divine glory) is divine/God‑given' — ایزدیst is the adjectival/nominal form with the archaic clitic -ست ('is'). The clause: 'He knew that that farr was divine.'
segments.s299-g94: از او = 'because of it' or 'from it' (giving the reason). بر تن خویش = 'upon his own body/self' (خویش written here split into خوی + ش but functions as the reflexive 'his own'). باید is the impersonal/modal 'it is necessary / must', and گریست is the infinitive/presentive 'to weep' (classical infinitival form). Together: 'for this he must weep on his own body' → 'he must weep for himself because of it.'
segments.s299-g95: همان = 'that same / that very'. اسبش is 'his horse' (اسب + possessive -ش). از تشنگی = causal phrase 'from/because of thirst'. شد غمی = 'became sorrowful/distressed' (شد + predicate noun غمی). This describes the horse's condition caused by thirst.
segments.s299-g96: به نیروی مرد = 'to/for the man's strength' (به + noun phrase indicating the recipient/thing affected). اندر آمد = 'came into' or 'entered' (i.e. 'was felt in'), and کمی is the adverb 'a little/slightly'. The whole clause means 'a little strength came to/returned to the man.'
segments.s299-g185: به + proper name + گفت is a prepositional dative + subject + past tense verb: 'به رهام گفت' = 'he said to Roham'. The following demonstrative phrase 'این بد بدسگال' is a noun phrase in apposition ('this ill‑fated / ill‑omened one'); the doubled 'بد' is a poetic intensifier and 'بدسگال' is an adjectival compound meaning 'ill‑omened'.
segments.s299-g186: This clause continues the description: 'دلیر و سبکسر' are two coordinated adjectives ('brave and light‑headed/rash') modifying the same person. The final sequence is a poetic inversion: 'م' + 'را' + 'بود' + 'خال' corresponds to 'مرا بود خال' = 'a mole was on me' / 'I had a mole'. Here 'بود' is the past/existential verb 'was', and 'خال' is the noun 'mole/spot'; the clitic 'م' + object marker 'را' together mark 'me' as the affected person.
segments.s299-g187: 'پس از کشتنش' is a temporal prepositional phrase: 'پس' ('then/after') + 'از' + the verbal noun/infinitive with enclitic object 'کشتنش' ('the killing of him' / 'killing him'). 'مهربانی کنید' is an imperative/plural (or polite) construction: noun 'مهربانی' (kindness/mercy) + auxiliary/verb 'کنید' (do/show). Together: 'پس از کشتنش مهربانی کنید' = 'After you kill him, show mercy.'
segments.s299-g188: 'یکی' here functions as an indefinite/indeterminate article ('a certain' / 'one'), followed by the direct object 'دخمه' (sepulchre/tomb) modified by the adjective 'خسروانی' (royal). The verb 'کنید' repeats the imperative 'make/build': 'Make a royal tomb.' Word order: quantifier + noun + adjective + verb (typical Persian ordering with the verb final).
segments.s299-g189: 'تنش را' = 'his body' (تن + enclitic ش + object marker را). The preposition 'به' introduces the instruments/materials used: 'به مشک و عبیر و گلاب' = 'with/ in (lit.) musk, ambergris and rosewater' — a list of aromatic substances to be used in washing the corpse. Note the clitic 'ش' marks possession ('his').
segments.s299-g190: 'بشویید' is the plural/polite imperative/subjunctive form of 'شستن' = 'wash'. 'مغزش' = 'his brain' (مغز + enclitic ش). 'به' again marks the material: 'به کافور ناب' = 'with/ in pure camphor' where 'ناب' is a postpositive adjective 'pure'. The verb applies to the immediately preceding object: 'Wash his brain with pure camphor.'
segments.s299-g191: 'به گردنش' = 'on/around his neck' (به + گردن + enclitic ش). 'بر طوق مشکین' = 'upon a musk‑scented collar' where 'بر' is a locative/prepositional particle 'on/upon', 'طوق' is 'collar/necklace' and 'مشکین' here describes it as musk‑scented (or perfumed with musk). 'نهید' is the 2pl/polite-present form of 'نهادن' used as an imperative: 'put/place.' Word order places locative phrase first for emphasis.
segments.s299-g192: 'کله بر سرش' = 'the head on/over his head' (کله + preposition بر + سر + enclitic ش) — idiomatically 'upon his head'. 'عنبرآگین' is a compound adjective meaning 'amber‑scented' or 'adorned with amber'. Again 'نهید' is the imperative 'place/put.' The structure is straightforward: object (head‑ornament) + locative phrase + adjective + imperative.
segments.s299-g9: چو آمد is an archaic/conjunctive clause meaning 'when ... came' (چو = 'when' + آمد past 'came'). The locative phrase به نزدیک ایران ('towards/near Iran') follows the verb, and the subject سپاه (army) appears after that: literal order 'when came toward near Iran the army' = 'when the army came near Iran.'
segments.s299-g10: یکی نامداری بشد is 'a certain famed one appeared/was made' — یکی is the indefinite 'a certain', نامداری here denotes a renowned/notable person, and بشد is the literary past-form meaning 'it happened / he became / he appeared'. نزد شاه places the figure 'in the presence of the king' (نزد = 'at/in the presence of').
segments.s299-g11: که آمد سواری begins a relative/explanatory clause: که = 'who/that', آمد past 'came', سواری = 'a rider/horseman'. میان دو صف is the locative 'between the two ranks' (میان = between, دو صف = two battle lines). The order is typical older Persian: relative + verb + noun + locative.
segments.s299-g12: سرافراز و جوشان و تیغی به کف is a series of modifiers describing that rider: سرافراز (proud/triumphant), جوشان (fiery/intense), and تیغی به کف is a fixed phrase 'with a sword in (his) hand' — تیغی here implies wielding a sword and به کف literally 'in the palm/hand'. The adjectives precede the sword-phrase in the descriptive string.
segments.s299-g13: بخندید از او شاه literally 'laughed (بخندید) at/over him (از او), the king (شاه)'; word order places the verb first (common in poetry) with the subject following. جوشن بخواست uses بخواست (past of خواستن) meaning 'he asked for/desired' — here the king requests the armor (جوشن).
segments.s299-g14: درفش بزرگی برآورد راست: درفش بزرگی ('a large/important banner') is the direct object phrase placed before the verb; برآورد is an older verbal form meaning 'brought forth/raised' here, and راست functions as 'upright/straight' (how he raised it). The clause is compact poetic word order: object + verb + manner.
segments.s299-g15: یکی ترگ زرین به سر بر نهاد: یکی = 'someone/a certain one', ترگ زرین = 'a golden cloth' (ترگ = a piece of cloth/rag here), and به سر بر نهاد is the compound past 'placed upon the head' (بر نهاد = placed on/upon). The prepositional phrase به سر marks the target location.
segments.s299-g16: درفشش به رهام گودرز داد shows older SOV order with the possessive enclitic -ش: درفشش = 'his banner' (درفش + -ش). به رهام marks the indirect object 'to Roham', and گودرز داد is the subject + past verb 'Goudarz gave'. So: 'Goudarz gave his banner to Roham.'
segments.s299-g33: شما is the emphatic 2nd-person plural/formal subject. هیچ دل را is the direct object: هیچ modifies دل ('no/any heart') and را marks it as definite/direct object. مدارید is a present-tense verb in 2nd-person (you) — here stating a habitual fact ('you do not / you never make'); تنگ is a resultative adjective/complement meaning 'narrow' (i.e., 'make (the heart) tight').
segments.s299-g34: چنینست is a copular/expository phrase 'it is thus' or 'such is'. آغاز و فرجام جنگ is a coordinated noun phrase ('the beginning and the end of war'); classical poetry often omits an ezāfe, so جنگ is read as the thing possessed/qualified by آغاز و فرجام. The clause as a whole comments on the previous clause.
segments.s299-g35: گهی ... گهی sets up an alternation ('sometimes ... sometimes ...'). بر فراز is a prepositional phrase 'on/upon the heights' (بر = on; فراز = summit/height). در نشیب is the contrasting prepositional phrase 'in the descent/low point' (در = in; نشیب = low place). Word order is typical poetic alternation with repeated گهی.
segments.s299-g36: Again گهی marks alternation. شادکامی is a noun 'happiness/joy' — the verb is omitted (elliptical), understood as 'there is/comes'. گهی با نهیب contrasts with the previous: با is the preposition 'with' and نهیب is a noun meaning 'rebuke' or 'a thunderous cry'; together 'sometimes with a rebuke.'
segments.s299-g37: برانگیخت is a past tense verb 'roused/awakened' (3rd singular). In this poetic order the verb comes first, followed by its subject شبرنگ and then the object بهزاد را (بهزاد marked by را as direct object). The sequence yields 'Shabrang roused Behzad.'
segments.s299-g38: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause 'that / since / for'. دریافتی is a past 2nd-person form 'you perceived/understood/learned'. روز تگ باد را is the object of دریافتی (را marks the whole phrase): روز = 'day'; تگ modifies باد as 'close/narrow' (i.e. 'close attack'); باد here is literal 'wind' used metaphorically for an onset/assault. The clause reads 'for you had learned/perceived the day of the close attack.'
segments.s299-g39: میان here means 'into the middle/midst'. بسته (literally 'bound/closed') in this construction describes having closed/made entry into the middle — a terse poetic way to say 'he closed into the ranks.' با introduces an instrumental/associative list: نیزه 'spear', خود 'himself', و گبر 'and a Gabr (a non‑Muslim/Zoroastrian companion)'.
segments.s299-g40: همی is a continuous/intensifying particle often used with past verbs in poetry (gives a sense like 'and then' / 'straightaway'). گرد here is the noun 'dust' and اسبش is 'his horse' (possessive -sh). گرد اسبش is the subject of the intransitive verb آمد 'came/rose up'; بر before آمد indicates upward motion, and به ابر is the prepositional goal 'to the cloud(s)'. The clause means 'the dust of his horse rose up to the clouds.'
segments.s299-g169: 'به هامون' is a prepositional phrase ('to/onto the plain') placed before the verb. 'چو پیلان' is a simile 'like elephants' modifying the verb that follows. The verb 'آویختند' is past plural and here, with the prefix-like particle 'بر', means 'fell upon' / 'fell onto' (an older/poetic use); word order is prepositional phrase + simile + verb.
segments.s299-g170: 'همی' is a narrative adverb giving a continuous or iterative sense ('kept / continuously'), placed before the clause. 'خاک با خون برآمیختند' is a past plural verb phrase: 'خاک' (earth/dust) is the subject/theme, 'با خون' ('with blood') the accompaniment, and 'برآمیختند' is the past tense verb 'mingled'/'blended' — so 'the earth was mingled with blood'.
segments.s299-g171: چو = temporal 'when'. 'شیده' is used as a noun/adjective 'the smitten/dazzled one' and functions as the subject. 'بدید' (past) is the verb 'saw'; the phrase 'آن بر و برز شاه' is the direct object meaning 'that high/exalted king' (بر و برز here convey lofty might/exaltation). Word order is subject + verb + object, typical of classical Persian.
segments.s299-g172: 'همان' is a demonstrative 'the same/that' referring back; 'ایزدی فر' is a noun phrase where 'ایزدی' (divine) qualifies 'فر' (farr/royal glory). The conjunction 'و' links to 'آن دستگاه' ('that court/establishment'). The clause lists what was seen: the divine royal-glory and the court.
segments.s299-g173: 'همی جست' is a habitual/continuous past — 'he kept leaping'. The clause 'کآید مگر ز او رها' contains 'کآید' (subjunctive/optative 'that he may come / may get away') and 'مگر' here introduces the sense 'perhaps / unless' (classical usages vary), followed by the prepositional phrase 'ز او رها' = 'free from him' ('ز' = from, 'او' = him, 'رها' = free). Together: 'he kept leaping as if perhaps he might free himself from him.'
segments.s299-g174: 'که' introduces an explanatory subordinate clause ('for' / 'because'). 'چون' = 'when'; 'سر بشد' = 'when the head was gone/severed' (بشد here = 'became/was'), and 'تن نیارد بها' = 'the body does not obtain / bring value' — literally, 'the body will not bring price/value.' The negative/resultative sense is that a headless body has no worth.
segments.s299-g175: چو = 'when'. 'آگاه شد' is a compound verb 'became aware' (آگاه + شد), with خسرو as the subject. 'از رای اوی' is a prepositional object 'of his counsel/plan' ('از' = of/about, 'رای' = counsel/plan, 'اوی' = his), so the whole means 'When Khosrow learned of his counsel.'
segments.s299-g176: 'وز' here is the fused classical form meaning 'and of' (originally و از) introducing another object. 'آن زور و آن برز' lists 'that strength/power and that exalted might/height', and 'بالای اوی' adds 'over him' or 'upon him' ('بالای' = above/upon, 'اوی' = his). Grammatically this is a coordination introduced by وز: 'and of that strength and that exaltation upon him.'
segments.s299-g225: This is a prepositional phrase with poetic word order: به سر بر (upon the head) + پراگند (scattered) + ریگ روان (flowing sand). The verb is omitted and understood from context (’they scattered/they threw’); ریگ روان is a compound where روان is an adjective meaning 'flowing'.
segments.s299-g226: ز لشکر برفت is 'departed/left from the army' (ز = 'from'), with برفت an archaic/simple past verb meaning 'went away/left'. آن‌که introduces the substantive relative 'the one who', and بد پهلوان is a predicate noun 'a bad/ill-suited champion' describing that person.
segments.s299-g227: رخ شاه ترکان is a genitive construction 'the face/visage of the king of the Turks'. هر آن کس که دید is a generic relative clause 'whoever saw (it)'; دید is past tense 'saw' and the object (the visage) is understood from the preceding noun.
segments.s299-g228: This lists the effects on observers: بر و جامه و دل همه بردرید — 'they tore (their) cloak and garment and heart (all)'. The repeated و links items in a coordinated list; همه emphasizes that all did this. بردرید is a past tense verb 'tore apart'. Note: بر here is a noun meaning 'cloak' (poetic use).
segments.s299-g229: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so'. گفت is the past verb 'said' (the lexeme/entry given is mismatched in the data, so its sense is not in the provided list). با مویه 'with lamentation' describes the manner, and افراسیاب is the subject placed after the verb (common in poetic inversion).
segments.s299-g230: کز این پس is an idiom 'from this on / henceforth' (کز = 'from/that', این پس = 'this after/after this'). نه ... نه ... is a correlative negation 'neither ... nor ...'. جویم is first-person present-subjunctive/preterbal form meaning 'I seek' or 'I will seek', so the phrase says 'I will seek neither peace (آرام) nor sleep (خواب)'.
segments.s299-g231: مرا اندر این سوگ یاری کنید is an imperative plea: مرا (me, marked by را as definite object) + اندر این سوگ 'in this mourning' + یاری کنید 'help (me)', where کنید is the plural imperative/subjunctive form 'do/help'. Note the data splits مرا into 'م' + 'را'; 'م' as listed does not match the actual pronoun entry, so its sense is marked accordingly.
segments.s299-g232: همه تن به تن سوگواری کنید: همه 'all/everyone' + تن به تن 'person by person / one by one' (an idiom built from 'تن' = person/body) + سوگواری کنید 'mourn' (imperative plural). The structure directs everyone individually to lament.
segments.s299-g73: بیابان is the head noun 'wilderness/desert'. The following clause is a relative clause introduced by که 'which/that'. آن is a demonstrative 'that' functioning inside the relative clause. از here is the partitive/genitive sense 'of/within', followed by در 'in' and رزم 'battle/field' to form 'of/in the field of battle'. بود is the past copula 'was'.
segments.s299-g74: بدان is a fused prepositional phrase (به + آن) meaning 'at/to that' and introduces the locative phrase جایگه 'place/spot'. مرز 'border/frontier' is the predicate noun, modified by the proper name خوارزم 'Khwarezm'. بود is the past copula 'was' completing the clause. (The token 'دان' as split in the word list does not transparently correspond to a separate meaning here.)
segments.s299-g75: رسیدند is the past plural 'they reached/arrived'. جایی is an indefinite locative 'a place/spot' and که introduces the relative clause 'where/that'. شیر و پلنگ are the coordinated subjects 'lion and leopard' of the relative clause.
segments.s299-g76: بدان is the locative 'to/at that' (به + آن). شخ is an uncertain/onomatopoeic term (treated here as a local hollow or exclamation). بی‑آب is an adjective 'without water/waterless' modifying شخ. ننهاد is the past of نهادن 'to put/place' (used here with the animals as subject), and چنگ is the object 'claw' (so literally 'did not place/put claw' — idiomatically 'did not strike with claws' or 'did not plant their claws').
segments.s299-g77: نپرید is the past of پریدن here 'he/it flew up, soared'. بر is the directional/prepositional 'into/upon', آسمانش is 'his/its sky' (possessive suffix), and عقاب is the subject/nominative 'eagle' that performs the flying.
segments.s299-g78: از او بهره‌ای is a small clause 'from it (or him) a share/thing' with بهره‌ای indefinite ('a sort of share/benefit'). شخ again appears as an onomatopoeic or uncertain noun (left untranslated here). The conjunction و links the following nominal phrase; بهری (from بهر) functions as 'another sort/portion' or 'as for another (thing)' and سراب is the noun 'mirage/illusion'. Note the copula is omitted — nominal phrases are juxtaposed in poetic style.
segments.s299-g79: نهادند is the past plural 'they placed/established'. آوردگاهی is a derived noun 'place of bringing' used idiomatically for 'battlefield/venue of battle', and بزرگ is the adjective 'large/great' modifying it. The verb governs the whole nominal phrase as its object.
segments.s299-g80: دو اسب و دو جنگی enumerates 'two horses and two warriors/fighters' (جنگی here nominal 'fighter'). به سان is a fixed comparative 'like/as', followed by دو گرگ 'two wolves' as the simile. Word order is straightforward: enumerated items + comparative phrase.
segments.s299-g49: اگر introduces a conditional; مغز here is used metaphorically as 'sense' or 'good judgment'. بودیت is an archaic/poetic second‑person singular past/conditional form ('you would have been' / 'had you been'), marking the protasis (the 'if' clause). با + خال خویش is a prepositional phrase: با 'with', خال 'blemish/mole', خوی + ش together form خویش ('one's own') via the possessive enclitic ش — grammatically this phrase modifies the reason or motive in the condition.
segments.s299-g50: نکردی is the negative past/conditional result (2nd person singular) tied to the preceding 'اگر' clause — here functioning as the counterfactual result 'you would not have done'. چنین modifies the following noun phrase ('such'). جنگ is the direct object and is followed by the object marker را; دست پیش is an idiomatic nominal phrase ('hand forward' = the initiative/first move) where دست is literal 'hand' used idiomatically and پیش indicates 'forward/in front'.
segments.s299-g51: اگر sets a conditional again. جنگ‌جویی is an agent noun 'a warrior' (indefinite). ز is the poetic preposition 'from'/'out of' often used where Modern Persian would use از; here ز پیش سپاه means 'in front of / before the host' or 'at the head of the army' — پیش + سپاه expresses position relative to the army.
segments.s299-g52: This line contains two imperatives. برو is the imperative 'go' and is followed by دور as an adverb/adverbial adjective meaning 'far/away' (go away). بگزین is the imperative 'choose'; یکی precedes the noun as an indefinite/partitive 'one/a certain', and رزمگاه is the object 'battlefield' — together: 'go away; choose a distant battlefield.'
segments.s299-g53: کز here functions as a conjunction introducing a subordinate/purpose clause ('so that' / 'that'); ایران و توران are coordinated place names. نبینند is a third‑person plural present/subjunctive form used optatively/jussively ('may they not see' / 'let no one see'), and کس is the object meaning 'anyone' but with the negation implied by نبینند it amounts to 'no one'. Note the verb preceding the object, a common word order in classical Persian.
segments.s299-g54: نخواهیم is first‑person plural present/optative from خواستن ('we will not want' / 'we do not desire') and serves as the main clause tied to the preceding purpose clause. یاران is the plural noun 'companions/comrades' (direct object), and فریادرس is a postpositive epithet 'rescuing' / 'one who comes to the cry' (i.e., 'rescuers'), qualifying یاران.
segments.s299-g55: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so' modifying the verb. داد is the third‑person singular past 'gave'. پاسخ is the direct object 'answer/reply' and بدو is the enclitic/adverbial 'to him' (indirect object). شهریار, though placed after these elements, is the subject 'the king' — classical Persian permits flexible word order so the subject can appear after verb and objects.
segments.s299-g56: که‌ای here functions as a vocative/exclamatory particle (classical 'که ای' → 'O!'); شیر درنده is a compound noun phrase 'ferocious lion' (شیر 'lion', درنده 'predatory/ferocious' as postpositive adjective). در کارزار is a prepositional phrase 'in (the) battle/field' where در is the preposition 'in' and کارزار is 'battle'. The whole is a vocative address: 'O ferocious lion in battle!'
segments.s299-g193: نگه کرد is a past verbal phrase 'looked/turned his gaze' (کرد is the past 'did' used with نگه). پس = 'then' (a temporal/sequence adverb). ترجمانش is 'his interpreter' (ترجمان + possessive suffix -ش). ز راه is a prepositional phrase 'from the road/from the way' that will attach to the motion/seeing verb in the next segment.
segments.s299-g194: بدید is the simple past 'he/they saw' and takes the following noun phrase as its object. آن = 'that' (demonstrative). تن here means 'person' (poetic use) and نامبردار is an epithet 'renowned/aforementioned' modifying تن; شاه apposes/identifies that person as 'the king'.
segments.s299-g195: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause ('which/that'). با خون is an adjunct meaning 'with blood' (instrumental/qualifying: 'stained with blood' or 'bearing blood'). از آن ریگ = 'from that pebble/sand' with از marking source; the noun ریگ here is best read as 'pebble'. برداشتند is the past plural 'they took/picked up/removed'.
segments.s299-g196: سوی = 'toward' (directional preposition). لشکر شاه = 'the king's army/host' (لشکر = army, شاه = king). بگذاشتند is past plural 'they placed / set (it)' — here the verb sends/places the mentioned object in the direction of the king's forces; subject is the same plural actor as in برداشتند.
segments.s299-g197: بیامد is the simple past 'he came/arrived'. خروشان is an adjectival/participial adverb 'roaring/tumultuous', describing the manner of coming. به نزدیک شاه = 'to/near the king' (به = to/toward, نزدیک = near, شاه = king) — a directional/dative phrase.
segments.s299-g198: که‌ای here functions as a vocative (older poetic form of 'ای', 'O' — addressing someone). نامور = 'renowned' modifying the addressee; دادگر = 'just' or 'just ruler' (an epithet); پیشگاه = 'presence' or 'court' — together they form a vocative phrase 'O famed, just one in the presence (of the court)'.
segments.s299-g199: یکی = 'a certain' / 'once' (indefinite introducer). بنده بودم = 'I was a servant' (بنده = servant/humble self‑reference; بودم = past 'I was'). من is an emphatic/explicit subject pronoun 'I'. او را نوان = 'to him (I was) young' — او را uses را to mark the person affected/related, and نوان is an adjective 'youthful/young'; the copula is supplied by the earlier بودم across the clause boundary.
segments.s299-g200: نه ... و نه ... is the correlative negation 'neither ... nor ...'. جنگی سواری = 'a warlike/martial rider' or 'fighting horseman' (جنگی qualifies military quality, سواری = rider/horseman). پهلوان = 'champion/hero'. The copula is omitted (understood from previous بودم); this lists qualities he lacked.
segments.s299-g145: This is a coordinated noun phrase listing three items. بدین (here written as ب + دین in the tokenization) is an archaic/prepositional determiner meaning 'by/with this' or 'such', modifying زور ('force/strength'). این فره is 'this farrah' (divine glory) and دستبرد is a further noun 'seizure/encroachment'. The three nouns are joined by the coordinating و 'and'.
segments.s299-g146: ندیدم is a past tense first-person negative verb 'I did not see'. به آوردگه is a prepositional phrase ('to/at the assembly or venue') with به marking direction/place. نیز adds 'also/even'. گرد here functions as an archaic verbal/participial element meaning roughly 'be present / occur / come about' (poetic usage), so the whole clause says that the speaker has not seen the listed things even at the assembly.
segments.s299-g147: ولیکن is a conjunction 'but/yet'. ستودان here is a noun (verbal noun) 'praise' and مرا (tokenized as م + را) is the object 'to me'/'of me' (م = me, را = definite object/topic marker) so ستودان مرا از گریز = 'praise of/for me on account of flight'. از introduces the cause/source ('from/for my flight').
segments.s299-g148: به آید is a present/poetic third-person construction 'comes (to me)'; به marks the recipient. چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. گیرم is a first-person present/subjunctive form of گرفتن 'I take/seize' (poetic/old form used for 'when I take up'). به کاری is 'to/into a work/task' (به marking direction or taking up), and ستیز is a noun 'strife/fighting' describing the kind of task. Together: 'praise comes when I take up a task of fighting.'
segments.s299-g149: هم 'even/also' gives emphasis. از گردش چرخ is a genitive/partitive phrase 'from the turning of the wheel' (چرخ = wheel/cycle, here the cosmic wheel of fate). بر بگذرم is a compound of the preposition بر + the verb بگذرم (first-person present/subjunctive/potential) meaning 'I pass over/beyond'. The clause therefore says the speaker can pass beyond the wheel's turning (i.e. beyond fate).
segments.s299-g150: وگر is the conditional 'but if / and if'. دیده اژدها is an object phrase 'the eye to the dragon' (دیده = eye, اژدها = dragon). بسپرم is a first-person_present/subjunctive form of سپردن 'I would entrust / I might hand over' (poetic possibility). The line offers an alternative extreme: if necessary, I would even give my eye to a dragon.
segments.s299-g151: گر 'if' introduces the condition. ایدر (اینجا) is the adverb 'here'. مرا (tokenized as م + را) is the object pronoun 'my/me' (م = me/my, را = object marker) placed before the noun in poetic word order; هوش is 'intelligence/sense/mind'. بر دست اوست is the idiom 'is on/in his hand' meaning 'is under his control' (بر = on/upon, دست = hand/power, او = he, ست = archaic copula 'is').
segments.s299-g152: This is a correlated negative construction introduced by نه ... نه 'neither ... nor'. دشمن ز من باز دارد literally 'an enemy from me [باز دارد]' — ز is the poetic form 'from', and باز دارد (باز داشتن) means 'to keep back / to restrain / to be held off' so the clause means 'no enemy will keep away from me' (i.e. enemies do not retreat). نه دوست continues the correlation 'nor (will) a friend' — the sense is that neither enemies nor friends will act to restrain or prevent (something) when my wit is in his hand. Word order is typical of classical Persian: subject/agent nouns and particles surround the verbal expression.
segments.s299-g209: خروشی بر آمد is a poetic expression where برآمد (بر + آمد) functions as a single verb meaning “rose up/arose,” with خروشی (a shout/roar) as its subject. ز ایران سپاه is a prepositional phrase 'from the Iranian host' (ز = from, ایران qualifies سپاه), and Persian allows the subject/phrase to follow or accompany the verb in this inverted, elevated word order.
segments.s299-g210: که introduces a subordinate/relative clause describing the previous shout. بخشایش is the object meaning 'forgiveness/mercy,' and آورد is past tense 'brought.' In this poetic line the verb appears before its subject خورشید و ماه (postverbal subject), so the clause reads literally 'that forgiveness brought the sun and moon' — i.e. 'and the sun and moon brought forgiveness.'
segments.s299-g211: بیامد همانگاه = 'came immediately.' بیامد is simple past 'he/they came' (poetic form), and همانگاه is an adverb 'at that instant/at once.' The names گودرز و گیو are the postverbal subjects (common in Persian poetry), listed after the verb and joined by و.
segments.s299-g212: چو is a simile particle 'as/like.' Here two similes are given: 'چو شیدوش' (like Shidush) and 'رستم چو گرگین نیو' (Rostam like Gorgin Niu). The line uses elliptical/condensed naming typical of epic verse; the repeated چو marks the comparisons. Proper names follow the simile markers and stand as nominal comparisons.
segments.s299-g213: همه بوسه دادند پیشش زمین is an idiomatic/poetic ordering: 'they all gave kisses to the earth before him' = 'they all kissed the ground in his presence.' بوسه دادند ('gave a kiss') is the verbal phrase; پیشش زمین marks the place/object ('the ground before him').
segments.s299-g214: بسی here functions as an adverbial intensifier 'much/a great deal' (poetic usage). شاه را is the definite object 'the king' (را marks the direct object). خواندند is past plural 'they called/sang/uttered' and here takes the sense 'to call out' (they cried aloud), while آفرین is the exclamation/word they uttered ('bravo' / 'acclamation').
segments.s299-g215: This phrase is compressed and poetic. وز آن روی ترکان likely means 'because/for that face/turn of the Turks' or 'from that side of the Turks' (وز here is an archaic/poetic preposition/particle and the exact sense is idiomatic). دو دیده به راه is an idiom meaning 'two eyes on the road' — i.e. people watching/waiting on the road. The verb 'were' is omitted (elliptical), which is common in epic verse.
segments.s299-g216: که introduces the content of their watching here as an indirect question/expectation. شیده is the referent being awaited (here the form could be a proper name or epithet; the lemma senses offered do not match a clear proper-name reading). کی is the interrogative 'when,' and آید is present/future sense 'will come' or 'comes' (classical present used for future reference), followed by ز آوردگاه 'from the battlefield' (ز = from, آوردگاه = battlefield).
segments.s299-g233: نه + verb here places a negative before the verb (typical poetic order): نه بیند = 'he will not see.' سر تیغ ما را is the object phrase: سر (lit. 'head' = 'tip') modifies تیغ ('sword'), ما is the possessor ('our') and را is the definite/object marker attached to the whole phrase. نیام (an archaic/poetic noun) means 'scabbard' and completes the object: literally 'he does not see the scabbard of our sword.'
segments.s299-g234: Parallel negative clause introduced by نه هرگز ('nor ever'); هرگز is a strong negation adverb. بوم is used in the sense 'homeland/native soil'; ز این سپس places the time/ground ('from this after/thereafter')—ز is the poetic form of 'از' and سپس = 'after/thereupon.' شادکام is the predicative adjective 'fortunate/happy.' Together: 'nor ever (will he be) happy in his homeland after this.'
segments.s299-g235: ز مردم شمر = 'Shamr from among the people' (ز marking origin/association). ار is the conditional 'if.' ز دام و دده literally 'from trap and dede/elder' — here ز again marks origin/association and و links the two nouns. This clause sets up a conditional/background: 'If Shamr is of the people, or from snares and the elder...' (the exact semantic role of دده is archaic/poetic).
segments.s299-g236: This is the main comment about the heart: دلی here names 'a heart' or 'a wholehearted/impulsive nature.' کاو in archaic poetry can function as a relative/pointing word ('that/whose' or 'who'), linking 'دلی' to the predicate; نباشد is a negative subjunctive/optative form ('is not / may not be'). به درد آژده is a prepositional phrase 'to/with respect to pain, startled/terrified' — آژده describes the startled/frightened state. Overall: '(a) heart that is not startled by pain.'
segments.s299-g237: مبادا introduces a strong negative wish/foresight ('may it not be / lest'). بدان is the fused/poetic combination of ب + دان functioning to point to 'that' (literally 'to that/of that'); دیده = 'eye' is the subject of the wish. در آب و شرم = 'in water and shame' (در = 'in', آب = 'water' as tears/metaphor, و = 'and', شرم = 'shame/modesty'). Together: 'May that eye not be in water and shame (i.e. may it not be filled with tears and shame).'
segments.s299-g238: که introduces a clause of explanation ('for' / 'because'). از درد ما = 'from/because of our pain' (ز/از marking cause); نیست = 'is not' (negation of existence/state). پر + خون گرم = 'full of warm blood' where پر is the adjective 'full (of)' and خون گرم 'hot/warm blood' (a figurative image). The clause explains the reason or corrects the expectation: '(for) it is not full of warm blood because of our pain.'
segments.s299-g239: از آن = 'from that.' ماه‌دیدار is a compound poetic epithet: literally 'moon-meeting' but here used as a kenning for the beloved/moon-faced person (i.e. 'moon-faced/beautiful one'). جنگی سوار = 'warlike/military rider' where جنگی qualifies the rider. The phrase lists origin/association and a description: 'from that moon-faced one, (comes) a warlike rider.'
segments.s299-g240: Another 'from that' phrase: از آن سروبن = 'from that cypress-like (person)' (سروبن is a poetic adjective/noun meaning cypress-like/tall person). بر لب جویبار = 'on the lip/brink of the stream' (بر = 'on/upon', لب = 'brink/edge' here, جویبار = 'stream'). The structure is parallel: 'from that cypress-like one, on the edge of the stream.'
segments.s299-g153: ندانم is the negated first‑person verb 'I do not know' (classical order: verb before pronoun for emphasis). من is an emphatic subject pronoun following the verb. این زور مردی ز چیست is a raised question phrase: زور 'strength' + مردی (here 'manliness' or 'manly power') and ز (poetic for از) + چی 'what' + ست (copula) implied — literally 'this manly strength is from what?'. The clause asks 'from what does this manly strength come?'.
segments.s299-g154: بر introduces the locus/possession 'upon' (literary use). این نامور فره is the noun phrase 'this famed/renowned farr (divine glory)'. ایزدیست is a fused predicate (ایزدی + است → 'is divine/of the gods'), so the segment reads literally 'upon this famed farr is divine' → 'this famed man is endowed with a divine farr'.
segments.s299-g155: پیاده is an adverbial/adjunct 'on foot'. مگر introduces a conditional/contrast ('unless' / 'if not'); the sequence دست یابم is a present‑subjunctive/optative 'may I reach/lay hands on' (یابم as subjunctive of یافتن/یافتن). دست here is literal 'hand' used idiomatically in دست یابیدن 'to get hold of'. بدوی in this position is problematic/archaic and does not match the provided senses (see senses field). The combined sense is 'unless I can reach him on foot...'.
segments.s299-g156: به پیکار is a directional phrase 'into/for battle' (به = 'into/towards'). خون اندر آرم is a verbal clause where خون is the object/theme ('blood') and اندر 'in' marks containment; آرم here is a first‑person verb meaning 'I will put/let (blood) into' but this particular lexical sense is not among the provided options (see senses). The final به جوی 'into the stream' repeats the directional به + noun. Overall: 'I will rush into battle and send blood into the stream.'
