segments.s299-g157: به + شیده is an indirect object: 'to Shideh' (shideh can be an epithet 'mad/crazed' or a proper name here). چنین گفت is the reporting phrase 'thus said' (چنین 'thus, so', گفت 'said'); in literary Persian the speaker (شاه جهان) follows the verb: 'thus said the king of the world.' The clause follows epic inversion where verb precedes its subject for rhythm and emphasis.
segments.s299-g158: که‌ای here functions as a vocative/interjection 'O (you)', addressing the addressee; نامدار is an epithet 'renowned'. از نژاد is a genitive/origin phrase 'of the lineage/seed', and مهان names the lineage/group (the precise cultural sense of مهان is specialized here and does not match the provided senses). The whole is the opening of direct speech: 'O renowned one, of the N‑lineage...'.
segments.s299-g159: ز تخم کیان means 'from the seed/stock of the Kians' (ز = poetic از 'from', تخم = 'offspring/stock', کیان = the Kayanian dynasty). بی‌گمان is an adverb 'undoubtedly'. کس نبود is a negative existential 'there was no one' (کس used here as 'no one', نبود = past 'was not'). Word order is emphatic/literary: origin phrase first, then the adverb, then the existential.
segments.s299-g160: که introduces a relative clause 'who/that'; هرگز 'never' negates the following verbal phrase. پیاده modifies نبرد آزمود: 'fought on foot' — پیاده 'on foot', نبرد 'battle/ fighting', آزمود (past participle/verb) 'tried/experienced' (here 'had ever tried/fought'). So the relative clause says 'who had ever fought on foot', negated by هرگز and the preceding نبود.
segments.s299-g17: This is a simple past-clause with the subject first (همه لشکرش, “all his army”) and a compound predicate at the end. زار and گریان are adjectival/participial predicates (‘lamenting’ / ‘weeping’) coordinated by و, and شدند (past of شدن) is the final verb meaning ‘became’/‘were’ that makes the adjectives into the resulting state.
segments.s299-g18: چو is the temporal conjunction ‘when’. بر آتش تیز is a prepositional phrase (‘upon/on the fierce/keen fire’), with the adjective تیز following the noun (poetic/normative Persian order). بریان is a past-participial adjective ‘burnt/roasted’ and شدند is the past auxiliary/verb ‘they became/were’, so the clause reads literally ‘when (they) were burnt on the fierce fire.’
segments.s299-g19: خروشی بر آمد is a verb phrase ‘a cry/roar arose’ (بر آمد = arose). The particle که here introduces the ensuing direct address/utterance, and که‌ای شهریار functions like ‘saying, “O king…”’ with ای شهریار as a vocative.
segments.s299-g20: به + noun phrase marks the beneficiary/target: به آهن تن خویش = ‘to/for your iron body’ (آهن تن = ‘iron-bodied’/armoured or ‘of iron’). خوی + ش together form خویش ‘his/your own’ (here split orthographically), and رنجه مدار is a fixed negative exhortation: رنجه ‘be distressed/suffer’ + مدار, an archaic prohibitive/optative particle meaning ‘do not’/‘may you not’ — together ‘do not distress/afflict (yourself).’
segments.s299-g21: شهان را (the kings, topicalized with را) + همه تخت (all the thrones) sets up the scene; بودی is the past copula/auxiliary ‘were/was’ and نشست is here the verb ‘sat’ (or ‘took their seats’) placed at the clause end, following classical Persian SOV order.
segments.s299-g22: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause (‘for/that’). بر کین is a prepositional phrase meaning ‘for/against vengeance’, کمر = ‘waist/loins’ and بر میان تو بست is literal ‘they bound (it) on/around your middle’ with بست (past of بستن) as the final verb. Word order places the prepositional phrases before the verb, as is normal in Persian narrative poetry.
segments.s299-g23: This is an optative/curse introduced by که + مباد. جز خاک تیره (‘except dark soil’) is the restrictive phrase modifying نشستش (‘his sitting/seat’), and مباد is an archaic optative negative meaning ‘may it not be.’ The whole clause expresses the wish that his seat be only dark earth (i.e. ruin/death).
segments.s299-g24: Another negative optative: به هیچ آرزو کام و دستش مباد = ‘may there be to him no fulfillment of any desire and no hand (help).’ به هیچ governs ‘no/none’; کام here is ‘fulfillment/satisfaction’ of desire and دست is ‘hand’ in the extended sense of help/power; مباد again is the negative optative ‘may it not be.’ The two nouns are coordinated by و and the possessive ش attaches to دست (and understood for کام).
segments.s299-g137: جز از + noun (= 'except for ...') begins the phrase; بازگشتن is the infinitive 'to return' and here names the sole course of action. ترا is the enclitic pronoun referring to 'you' (beneficiary/target: 'for you'), and رای 'counsel/plan' is the noun governed by نیست — the negative existential 'there is not'. Word order is poetic but reads: 'Except for returning, for you there is no counsel.'
segments.s299-g138: که introduces the reason/explanation ('for / because'). با + جنگ = 'in/with battle'; خسرو is the proper name Khosrow. ترا again marks 'you' (the one concerned). پای here means 'foot/base' used metaphorically as 'standing, footing', and نیست states the lack of that footing. The clause explains why there is no counsel: you have no standing in battle with Khosrow.
segments.s299-g139: به هنگام is a temporal phrase 'at the right time / in due season'; کردن here is the light verb forming a verbal noun phrase ('the acting/doing at the right time'); ز + دشمن = 'from the enemy'; گریز is the noun 'flight/escape'. Together: 'a timely flight from the enemy.'
segments.s299-g140: به از is a comparative idiom 'better than'. کشتن and جستن are infinitives 'to kill' (here 'to be killed/die') and 'to seek' respectively. رستخیز is 'resurrection' (or rising again); the clause contrasts a timely flight with dying and hoping for resurrection.
segments.s299-g141: بدان is the imperative 'know/consider' (be aware that ...); نامور ترجمان شیده is a noun phrase 'the famed interpreter Shideh' (نامور = renowned, ترجمان = interpreter/expounder, شیده here a name/adjective). گفت is the reporting verb 'said' in past tense — the clause therefore introduces a reported speech introduced by که in the next segment.
segments.s299-g142: که is the subordinating conjunction 'that' introducing the reported content. آورد is the verb 'to bring' (3rd-person), مردان is the object 'men' (or 'heroes'), نشاید is an impersonal negative 'it is not fitting/should not', and نهفت is the participle/adjective 'hidden/concealed' — together: 'that bringing men it is not fit to hide' (= 'that one should not conceal bringing of men').
segments.s299-g143: چنان دان is an idiom 'consider it thus/know this' (چنان = 'so', دان = imperative 'know/consider'). که introduces the subordinate clause. تا = 'until'; من = 'I' is explicit subject; ببستم is the past/subjunctive form of 'بستن' here meaning 'I tie/gird' (part of the idiom کمر بستن = 'gird one's loins / prepare for action'); کمر is 'belt/girdle' (here 'girding the loins').
segments.s299-g144: همی is a verbal particle marking continuous/future action here; برفرازم is a verb form meaning 'I will raise/highen' (بر + فروز/فراز — 'lift up/exalt'). به introduces the object/direction 'toward', خورشید = 'the sun' (literal or metaphorical splendor), and سر 'head' (i.e. 'I will lift my head to/above the sun'). The line expresses resolve: once prepared, the speaker will exalt himself.
segments.s299-g57: منم is the fused copula/pronoun 'I am' (first-person declarative). داغ‌دل is a compound adjective 'heart-sore / grief-stricken' that modifies پور ('son'); پور + آن بیگناه forms a genitive description 'son of that innocent (one)'. Word order places the speaker (منم) before the noun phrase for emphasis.
segments.s299-g58: This is a relative clause introduced by که ('who/that'). شد + کشته is a periphrastic passive: شد (auxiliary 'was') + past participle کشته ('killed/slain') = 'was slain'. بر دست شاه gives the agent/instrumental phrase 'by the hand of the king' (بر + دست + شاه). The clause modifies 'آن بیگناه' (the innocent one).
segments.s299-g59: بدین دشت = 'to this plain' (بدین = to/for this, followed by دشت 'plain'). از ایران marks source 'from Iran'. به کین آمدم is the verb phrase 'I came for vengeance' — آمدم is first-person past 'I came', and به کین ('for vengeance') expresses purpose. Word order places the locative/goal and source before the verb, typical in Persian narrative.
segments.s299-g60: نه (negation) + از بهر is an archaic/poetic prepositional phrase meaning 'for the sake of' (not 'because of' here: 'not for the sake of...'). گاه 'time/position' and نگین 'gem/jewel' are the two things denied as motives. آمدم repeats the verb 'I came' for contrast. The phrase therefore means 'I did not come for rank/position or for jewels.'
segments.s299-g61: ز پیش پدر = 'from before (the) father' (ز as poetic 'from', پیش 'before/in the presence of', پدر 'father'). چون که introduces a temporal clause 'when/after' and is followed by برخاستی, second-person past 'you rose/left/ departed'. Together: 'When you departed from your father's presence...'.
segments.s299-g62: ز لشکر = 'from the army' (z = from). نبرد here is a noun 'battle' giving the purpose/context; مرا (object marker را attached to م in the tokenization) marks 'me' as direct object, and خواستی is second-person past 'you wanted/asked for'. The literal order places the purpose noun and object before the verb: 'from the army you asked me for battle.'
segments.s299-g63: مرا خواستی = 'you sought/asked for me' (مرا = object 'me' with را, خواستی = 'you wanted/asked' past). کس نبودی روا = 'there was no one fitting' (کس = anyone/person, نبودی = past negative 'there was not', روا = 'proper/fitting'). The two clauses are juxtaposed: 'You asked for me; there was no one proper.'
segments.s299-g64: که introduces a subordinate/relative clause 'that/whom'. پیشت = 'before you / in your presence' (پیش + enclitic ت). فرستادمی is an archaic/poetic verb form with object-before-verb order meaning 'I sent' (the older ordering places the object before the verb), and ناسزا is the object 'insult/abuse/reproach'. The clause thus says roughly 'whom I sent before you as an insult/reproach' or 'that I sent to you in disgrace.'
segments.s299-g129: ترا (ت + را) is the object pronoun 'you' with the accusative/indirect-object clitic را attached; placed before the predicate for emphasis. نیز 'also' adds the sense 'too'. با رزم او is a prepositional phrase 'with/ in the fighting of him' (i.e. 'in fighting him'). پای نیست is a predicate: پای 'foot/standing/footing' + نیست 'is not' = 'has no footing/standing'. The overall clause states that the addressee lacks standing to fight that man.
segments.s299-g130: به ترکان opens a domain/location phrase 'among / for the Turks' (به here marks the sphere). چون او is a comparative 'like him'. لشکر آرای is a compound nominal phrase: لشکر 'army' + آرای (from آراستن) 'army-adorner/one who arrays an army' — i.e. a man who can muster/array troops. نیست negates the whole copular idea: 'there is not (one) like him among the Turks.'
segments.s299-g131: یکی is an indefinite/introducing particle 'one / a certain'. مرد جنگی is a noun with postpositive modifier: مرد 'man' + جنگی 'warlike / fighter' = 'a warlike man' or 'a warrior'. فریدون نژاد is a genitival/attributive compound 'of Fereydun's stock/lineage' (نژاد = lineage). The phrase introduces a specific warrior of distinguished pedigree.
segments.s299-g132: که is the relative pronoun 'who/that' linking to the preceding 'مرد'. چون او 'like him' makes a comparison. دلاور is an adjective/noun 'valiant, brave'. ز مادر نزاد is a poetic word order: ز (from) + مادر (mother) + نزاد (past/predicative form from زادن 'to bear / give birth') = 'was born from his mother'; here نزاد is an archaic/poetic past or perfective form meaning 'was born' or 'came into being'. The clause says he, like that other, was born brave of his mother.
segments.s299-g133: نباشد is a negative optative/impersonal verbal form (literary) meaning 'there would not be' / 'it would not be'. مرا (م + را) is the dative/accusative clitic 'me' (for me). ننگ 'shame' is the nominal subject of the existential construction; رفتن is the masdar/verbal noun 'going' (i.e. 'to go'). به جنگ is the purpose/destination phrase 'to battle/into war'. The idiom literally reads 'there will not be to me shame of going to battle' = 'it is no shame for me to go to war.'
segments.s299-g134: پیاده is an adverb/adjective 'on foot / infantry-wise'. بسازیم is 1st-person-plural hortative/subjunctive 'let us make/let us arrange'. جنگ پلنگ is the direct object: جنگ 'battle' + پلنگ (here an epithet/nickname, 'Panther'/'Leopard') — either describing the fight or addressing someone as 'O Panther'. The clause is a hortative: 'let us make the fight on foot (O Panther).' Note the hortative verb ends the clause as is common in classical style.
segments.s299-g135: وزان سو is a frozen/poetic prepositional phrase 'from that side/direction' (وزان = 'from that'; سو = 'side/direction'). بر شیده is the phrase 'to Shideh' (بر = toward/to; شیده here is the proper name Shideh — formally identical to the adjective 'love-struck', but here a name). شد is the past auxiliary/verb 'happened / came about' and ترجمان 'interpreter / envoy' is the subject; order is verb then subject (inversion) as common in poetry: 'an interpreter came to Shideh from that side.'
segments.s299-g136: که introduces reported speech or the content of what the envoy said: 'that/that (he said)'. دوری گزین is a short hortative/imparative: دوری 'distance/avoidance' + گزین 'choose' = 'choose distance' → 'keep away'. از introduces the object of avoidance 'بد بدگمان' — literally 'bad (بد) suspicious (بدگمان)', i.e. 'the ill-suspecting / the maliciously suspicious'. The clause is the envoy's admonition: 'Keep away from the evil/suspicious.'

sentences.s299-s53: He said to him, “O king, let each man fight with sword and spear and take up (turn) the reins.”
sentences.s299-s54: Let him come on foot so that we may seek battle; in the manner of lions we will burst forth with our claws.
sentences.s299-s55: The sovereign Khosrow, too, at that time perceived a suspicious thought.
sentences.s299-s56: He said to his heart that this lion, by strength and claw, is a scion of Fereydun and a son of Pashang.
sentences.s299-s125: Let us set the whole army's hearts roaring, and bring them to the audience-hall, strewing (their) heads upon the place of arrival.
sentences.s299-s126: There had been no grudge against Khosrow before this; now he added hatred upon hatred as well.
sentences.s299-s127: The army became broken‑hearted for the king; the whole battlefield was roaring and seething.
sentences.s299-s49: When he became desperate he pondered in his heart whether he should tell the king in the battle.
sentences.s299-s50: Come, so that we may disembark onto the ship and both of us be made disciplined in our natures.
sentences.s299-s51: He should not go on foot, for shame will come upon him; from his kingship his body will be humbled.
sentences.s299-s52: By such a remedy I cannot find release from him; I became, without doubt, in the dragon's breath.
sentences.s299-s89: He seized him by the left of the neck and the right of the back, lifted him up and struck him hard upon the ground.
sentences.s299-s90: All the vertebrae of his back became like a reed, scattering with pain, and the connection (spine) broke apart.
sentences.s299-s91: Someone drew a sharp sword and tore the renowned heart through and through.
sentences.s299-s92: His coat of mail upon him was all split and torn; dust (earth) poured upon his crown from pain.
sentences.s299-s33: Now wish for a single battlefield — a rendezvous far from the midst of the host.
sentences.s299-s34: They made a covenant that from either side no vindictive person shall come to aid.
sentences.s299-s35: Also those who bear the banner with us — because of their ill disposition their banner shall not turn purple.
sentences.s299-s36: Both departed away from the army to a distance, just as a man becomes joyful at a feast.
sentences.s299-s61: If one must set foot on the earth, I am of the stock of Keshvad.
sentences.s299-s62: Stay then until I become his war‑maker, not a proud world‑ruling king.
sentences.s299-s63: Then the king said to Roham: 'O kind champion on horseback...'
